content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob play a game on an array of NNN integers. They alternate moves, with Alice making the first move.

The rules are as follows:


On **their first move**, a player can pick **any** element in the array, add its value to their score, and then **remove** it from the array.
On a move that is **not their first move**, the player should pick an element with the **opposite parity** of the element chosen on **their previous move**, add its value to their score, and then **remove** it from the array.
If a player cannot make a move, either because the array is empty or it doesn't contain an element of the parity they need, the player skips their turn.
The game ends when both players are unable to move.
Note that the parity of an element chosen by a player depends only on the parity of their own previously chosen element — it does not depend on what their opponent last chose.

Determine the optimal score of Alice if both players play optimally, each attempting to **maximize** their own score. If there are multiple ways to obtain maximum score for themselves, the players will adopt a strategy that will **maximize** the score of their opponent whilst obtaining their maximum score themselves.

Note that it is not necessary to use all the elements in the array.

Also note that when a player skips a turn, the parity that he needs in the next move remains same as that of the current move.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.
The first line of each test case contains one integer NNN — the size of the array AAA
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers, denoting the array elements.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the optimal score achieved by Alice.
constraints: Sum of NNN across all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Under optimal play, Alice's maximum score is 555. One sequence of moves leading to this score is:

On her first move, Alice picks 444.
On his first move, Bob picks 333.
On her second move, Alice must choose an odd number, since she previously picked an even number. So, Alice's only choice is to pick 111.
On his second move, Bob picks 222, since he must choose an even number and it is the only one left.

Alice's score is thus 4+1=54 + 1 = 54+1=5. Note that this is not the only optimal strategy — however, any other strategy where both players play optimally will still lead to Alice having a score of 555.
Test case 222: Alice's strategy is as follows:

On her first move, Alice picks 101010.
On his first move, Bob picks 535353.
Finally, Alice picks 454545, ending up with a score of 10+45=5510 + 45 = 5510+45=55.

Note that if Alice picks either 535353 or 454545 on her first move, Bob will pick 101010 on his move. This leaves Alice unable to make a move, so she skips her turn and Bob picks the remaining number, leaving Alice with a score strictly smaller than 555555.
Test case 333: Alice picks 6,7,6, 7,6,7, and 222 for a score of 151515, while Bob picks 2,5,2, 5,2,5, and 222 for a score of 999. Note that Bob can also achieve a score of 999 by picking 777 and 222 and then skipping his third move, but he won't do this because it would lead to Alice only reaching a score of 131313 — as mentioned earlier, a player attempts to maximize their own score, and then maximize their opponent's score.
